From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1960 British film by Karel Reisz

| Saturday Night and Sunday Morning | |
| --- | --- |
| Original British quad format cinema poster | |
| Directed by | [Karel Reisz](/wiki/Karel_Reisz "Karel Reisz") |
| Screenplay by | [Alan Sillitoe](/wiki/Alan_Sillitoe "Alan Sillitoe") |
| Based on | *[Saturday Night and Sunday Morning](/wiki/Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning "Saturday Night and Sunday Morning")* 1958 novel by Alan Sillitoe |
| Produced by | [Tony Richardson](/wiki/Tony_Richardson "Tony Richardson") |
| Starring | [Albert Finney](/wiki/Albert_Finney "Albert Finney") [Shirley Anne Field](/wiki/Shirley_Anne_Field "Shirley Anne Field") [Rachel Roberts](/wiki/Rachel_Roberts_(actress) "Rachel Roberts (actress)") [Hylda Baker](/wiki/Hylda_Baker "Hylda Baker") [Norman Rossington](/wiki/Norman_Rossington "Norman Rossington") |
| Cinematography | [Freddie Francis](/wiki/Freddie_Francis "Freddie Francis") |
| Edited by | [Seth Holt](/wiki/Seth_Holt "Seth Holt") |
| Music by | [John Dankworth](/wiki/John_Dankworth "John Dankworth") |
| Production company | [Woodfall Film Productions](/wiki/Woodfall_Film_Productions "Woodfall Film Productions") |
| Distributed by | [Bryanston Films](/wiki/Bryanston_Films_(UK) "Bryanston Films (UK)") (UK) [Continental Distributing](/wiki/Continental_Distributing "Continental Distributing") (USA) |
| Release dates | * 27 October 1960 (1960-10-27) (UK) * 3 April 1961 (1961-04-03) (US)[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Running time | 89 minutes |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Language | English |
| Budget | £116,848[[2]](#cite_note-money-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie20177-3) or £120,420[[4]](#cite_note-4) |
| Box office | £401,825 (UK) (as of 31 Dec 1964)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie20179-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) |

***Saturday Night and Sunday Morning*** is a 1960 British [kitchen sink](/wiki/Kitchen_sink_realism "Kitchen sink realism") drama film directed by [Karel Reisz](/wiki/Karel_Reisz "Karel Reisz") and produced by [Tony Richardson](/wiki/Tony_Richardson "Tony Richardson").[[7]](#cite_note-BFIsearch-7) It is an adaptation of the [1958 novel](/wiki/Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning "Saturday Night and Sunday Morning") by [Alan Sillitoe](/wiki/Alan_Sillitoe "Alan Sillitoe"), with Sillitoe himself writing the screenplay. The plot concerns a young [teddy boy](/wiki/Teddy_Boy "Teddy Boy") machinist, Arthur, who spends his weekends drinking and partying, all the while having an affair with a married woman.

The film is one of a series of "[kitchen sink drama](/wiki/Kitchen_sink_realism "Kitchen sink realism")" films made in the late 1950s and early 1960s, as part of the [British New Wave](/wiki/British_New_Wave "British New Wave") of filmmaking, from directors such as Reisz, [Jack Clayton](/wiki/Jack_Clayton "Jack Clayton"), [Lindsay Anderson](/wiki/Lindsay_Anderson "Lindsay Anderson"), [John Schlesinger](/wiki/John_Schlesinger "John Schlesinger"), and Tony Richardson, and adapted from the works of writers such as Sillitoe, [John Braine](/wiki/John_Braine "John Braine"), and [John Osborne](/wiki/John_Osborne "John Osborne"). A common trope in these films is the working-class "[angry young man](/wiki/Angry_young_men "Angry young men")" character (in this case, the character of Arthur), who rebels against the oppressive social and economic systems established by previous generations.

In 1999, the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") named *Saturday Night and Sunday Morning* the 14th greatest British film of all time on its list of the [Top 100 British films](/wiki/BFI_Top_100_British_films "BFI Top 100 British films").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Arthur Seaton is a young machinist at the [Raleigh](/wiki/Raleigh_Bicycle_Company "Raleigh Bicycle Company") bicycle factory in [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"). He is determined not to be tied down to living a life of domestic drudgery like the people around him, including his parents, whom he describes as "dead from the neck up". He spends his wages at weekends on drinking and having a good time.

Arthur is having an affair with Brenda, the wife of Jack, who works at the same factory as Arthur. He also begins a more traditional relationship with Doreen, a beautiful single woman closer to his age. Doreen, who lives with her mother and aspires to be married, avoids Arthur's sexual advances, so he continues to see Brenda as a sexual outlet.

Brenda becomes pregnant by Arthur, who offers to help raise the child or terminate the unwanted pregnancy ([abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abortion in the United Kingdom") not being legal in Britain at the time the film takes place). Seeking advice, he takes her to see his Aunt Ada, who has Brenda sit in a hot bath and drink gin, which does not work. Brenda asks Arthur for £40 to get an abortion from a doctor.

After Doreen complains about not going anywhere public with Arthur, he takes her to the fair, where he sees Brenda with her family. When he manages to get Brenda alone, she reveals that she has decided to have the child, and then leaves Arthur to go back to her family before she is missed. Arthur follows her on to an amusement ride and gets in a car with her. Brenda's brother-in-law and his friend—both of whom are soldiers—notice her enter the ride and, following, catch Arthur riding with his arm around Brenda. Arthur escapes the ride, but the soldiers catch him later and beat him up in a vacant lot.

Arthur spends a week recovering from his injuries. Doreen visits him, and he makes the moves on her, but is interrupted by his cousin, Bert; they finally consummate their relationship later, at her house, after her mother goes to sleep. Back at work, Arthur runs into Jack, who tells him to stay away from Brenda, as they are staying together. Although he still has mixed feelings about settling into domestic life, Arthur decides to marry Doreen, and, on a hill overlooking a new housing development, they discuss their differing ideas of what their life together will look like.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Albert Finney](/wiki/Albert_Finney "Albert Finney") as Arthur Seaton
* [Shirley Anne Field](/wiki/Shirley_Anne_Field "Shirley Anne Field") as Doreen
* [Rachel Roberts](/wiki/Rachel_Roberts_(actress) "Rachel Roberts (actress)") as Brenda
* [Hylda Baker](/wiki/Hylda_Baker "Hylda Baker") as Aunt Ada
* [Norman Rossington](/wiki/Norman_Rossington "Norman Rossington") as Bert
* [Bryan Pringle](/wiki/Bryan_Pringle "Bryan Pringle") as Jack
* [Robert Cawdron](/wiki/Robert_Cawdron "Robert Cawdron") as Robboe
* Edna Morris as Mrs Bull
* [Elsie Wagstaff](/wiki/Elsie_Wagstaff "Elsie Wagstaff") as Mrs Seaton
* [Frank Pettitt](/wiki/Frank_Pettitt "Frank Pettitt") as Mr Seaton
* [Avis Bunnage](/wiki/Avis_Bunnage "Avis Bunnage") as Blousy Woman
* [Colin Blakely](/wiki/Colin_Blakely "Colin Blakely") as Loudmouth
* Irene Richmond as Doreen's Mother
* Louise Dunn as Betty
* Anne Blake as Civil Defence Officer
* Frank Smith as Himself
* [Peter Madden](/wiki/Peter_Madden_(actor) "Peter Madden (actor)") as Drunken Man
* [Cameron Hall](/wiki/Cameron_Hall_(actor) "Cameron Hall (actor)") as Mr Bull
* [Alister Williamson](/wiki/Alister_Williamson "Alister Williamson") as Police Constable
* [Peter Sallis](/wiki/Peter_Sallis "Peter Sallis") as Man in Suit (uncredited)
* [Jack Smethurst](/wiki/Jack_Smethurst "Jack Smethurst") as Waiter (uncredited)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Style")]

*Saturday Night and Sunday Morning* was at the forefront of the [British New Wave](/wiki/British_New_Wave "British New Wave"), with its serious portrayal of [British working class](/wiki/British_working_class "British working class") life and realistic handling of topics such as sex and abortion.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It was among the first of the "[kitchen sink dramas](/wiki/Kitchen_sink_realism "Kitchen sink realism")" that followed the success of the 1956 play *[Look Back in Anger](/wiki/Look_Back_in_Anger "Look Back in Anger")*. Producer [Tony Richardson](/wiki/Tony_Richardson "Tony Richardson") later directed another such film, *[The Loneliness of the Long Distance Runner](/wiki/The_Loneliness_of_the_Long_Distance_Runner_(film) "The Loneliness of the Long Distance Runner (film)")* (1962), which was also adapted from an Alan Sillitoe [book of the same name](/wiki/The_Loneliness_of_the_Long-Distance_Runner_(collection) "The Loneliness of the Long-Distance Runner (collection)").

The film received an [X rating](/wiki/X_rating "X rating") from the [BBFC](/wiki/British_Board_of_Film_Classification "British Board of Film Classification") upon its theatrical release. It was re-rated PG before a 1990 home video release.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Filming locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filming locations")]

Much of the exterior location filming took place in [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), but some scenes were shot elsewhere. For example, the night scene with a pub named The British Flag in the background was filmed along Culvert Road in [Battersea](/wiki/Battersea "Battersea"), London, the pub being at the junction of Culvert Road and Sheepcote Lane (now Rowditch Lane).

According to an article that appeared in the *Nottingham Evening News* on 30 March 1960, the closing scene showing Arthur and Doreen on a grassy slope overlooking a housing development that is still under construction was shot in Wembley, with the assistance of Nottingham builders Simms Sons & Cooke.

### Financing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Financing")]

Bryanston guaranteed £81,820 of the budget, the [NFFC](/wiki/National_Film_Finance_Corporation "National Film Finance Corporation") provided £28,000, Twickenham Studios provided £610, and Richardson deferred his producers fee of £965. The film went £3,500 over budget and two days over schedule when filming at a Nottingham factory proved more difficult than expected.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie20179-5) [Woodfall](/wiki/Woodfall_Film_Productions "Woodfall Film Productions") bought the rights to the novel from Joseph Janni for £2,000, and got Sillitoe to do the script because they could not afford a professional screenwriter.[[12]](#cite_note-sue-12)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reception")]

*Saturday Night and Sunday Morning* opened at the [Warner cinema](/wiki/Vue_West_End "Vue West End") in London's West End on 27 October 1960, and received generally favourable reviews. It entered general release on the [ABC cinema circuit](/wiki/ABC_Cinemas "ABC Cinemas") from late January 1961, and was a box-office success, being the third most popular film in Britain that year.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

It earned a profit of over £500,000,[[14]](#cite_note-tino-14) with Bryanston earning a profit of £145,000.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie20179-5)

After viewing the film, [Ian Fleming](/wiki/Ian_Fleming "Ian Fleming") sold the rights to the [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") series to the film's executive producer [Harry Saltzman](/wiki/Harry_Saltzman "Harry Saltzman"), who, with [Albert R. "Cubby" Broccoli](/wiki/Albert_R._Broccoli "Albert R. Broccoli"), would co-produce every James Bond film between *[Dr. No](/wiki/Dr._No_(film) "Dr. No (film)")* (1962) and *[The Man with the Golden Gun](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Gun_(film) "The Man with the Golden Gun (film)")* (1974).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Awards")]

| [Best British Film](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_British_Film "BAFTA Award for Best British Film") | Won |

| [Best British Actress](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_British_Actress "BAFTA Award for Best British Actress") | [Rachel Roberts](/wiki/Rachel_Roberts_(actress) "Rachel Roberts (actress)") | Won |
| [Best British Screenplay](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_British_Screenplay "BAFTA Award for Best British Screenplay") | [Alan Sillitoe](/wiki/Alan_Sillitoe "Alan Sillitoe") | Nominated |

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Arthur Seaton is mentioned in the song "Where Are They Now?" by [The Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks"), which appears on their 1973 album *[Preservation Act 1](/wiki/Preservation_Act_1 "Preservation Act 1")*, and in the 1984 song "From Across the Kitchen Table" by [The Pale Fountains](/wiki/The_Pale_Fountains "The Pale Fountains").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The run-out groove on the B-side of vinyl copies of [The Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths")' 1986 album *[The Queen Is Dead](/wiki/The_Queen_Is_Dead "The Queen Is Dead")* features the line "Them was rotten days," a line said by Aunt Ada in the film. "Why don't you ever take me where's it lively and there's people?", a line said by Doreen before Arthur takes her to the fair, inspired the song "[There Is a Light That Never Goes Out](/wiki/There_Is_a_Light_That_Never_Goes_Out "There Is a Light That Never Goes Out")" on the same album ("I want to see people and I want to see life").[[20]](#cite_note-smiths-20) [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey"), the lead singer and lyricist of The Smiths, has stated that the film is one of his favourites.[[20]](#cite_note-smiths-20)

[The Stranglers](/wiki/The_Stranglers "The Stranglers")' live album *[Saturday Night, Sunday Morning](/wiki/Saturday_Night,_Sunday_Morning "Saturday Night, Sunday Morning")* is named after the film.

"Saturday Night Sunday Morning" is the title of a song on [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)")' 1999 album *[Wonderful](/wiki/Wonderful_(Madness_album) "Wonderful (Madness album)")*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The title of indie rock band [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys")' 2006 debut album, *[Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not](/wiki/Whatever_People_Say_I_Am,_That%27s_What_I%27m_Not "Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not")*, comes from a monologue Arthur delivers in the film.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The promotional video for the 2013 [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Franz_Ferdinand_(band) "Franz Ferdinand (band)") single "[Right Action](/wiki/Right_Action "Right Action")" uses elements of the graphic design from the film's original cinema poster, and some of the song's lyrics were inspired by a postcard addressed to [Karel Reisz](/wiki/Karel_Reisz "Karel Reisz") that the band's lead singer, [Alex Kapranos](/wiki/Alex_Kapranos "Alex Kapranos"), found for sale on a market stall.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The film is referenced in *[Torvill and Dean](/wiki/Torvill_%26_Dean_(film) "Torvill & Dean (film)")*, a 2018 biopic of Nottingham [ice dancers](/wiki/Ice_Dance "Ice Dance") [Jayne Torvill](/wiki/Jayne_Torvill "Jayne Torvill") and [Christopher Dean](/wiki/Christopher_Dean "Christopher Dean"). Like Arthur, Jayne's father works in the [Raleigh](/wiki/Raleigh_Bicycle_Company "Raleigh Bicycle Company") factory, and when a young Jayne mentions the film, her mother scolds her and calls the film "rude".
